caveats: a: The occupation mix is for a broader durable-goods wholesale grouping, not six-digit 423830.
a: Vendor case-study productivity figures may reflect successful deployments and not the average firm.
a: Exposure is to tasks and avoidable hiring, not whole jobs.
rho: Published surveys mix distributor verticals and company sizes.
rho: Intent to deploy is not completed implementation.
rho: Successful examples may suffer selection and sponsor bias.
e: Service revenue is not identical to recurring outsourced distribution activity.
e: The qualifying share can vary sharply by machinery category and aftermarket mix.
e: The injected firm count is an estimate and may include project-oriented dealers.
s5: Owner intentions do not equal closed transfers.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and includes much smaller employer firms.
s5: BizBuySell overrepresents brokered small deals and does not report a six-digit denominator.
q: No source directly measures five-year AI-benefit retention.
q: Retention differs between proprietary engineered lines and readily comparable products.
q: Vendor rebates and inventory gains can change capture independently of customer pricing.
d5: BLS output is for all wholesale trade, not 423830.
d5: CBO investment includes structures and intellectual property as well as equipment.
d5: The range excludes nominal tariff-driven price changes by construction.
o: Operator-required does not mean every current manual role remains.
o: OEM channel conflict differs by vendor agreements and product complexity.
o: The D2C survey covers suppliers across distribution verticals.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Industrial Machinery and Equipment Merchant Wholesalers

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily product gross margin on recurring and project orders from manufacturers, warehouses, oil and gas operators, and other industrial customers, with some shipping, installation, kitting, engineering, inventory-management, and aftermarket service fees.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturers, brokers that never take title, captive distribution units, one-off asset liquidators, pure rental businesses, and firms whose economics depend primarily on manufacturing or contracting rather than merchant wholesale distribution.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market industrial machinery and equipment distributors serving external business customers through repeat equipment, parts, consumables, MRO, technical-support, inventory-management, and related fulfillment relationships.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation counts for the broader durable-goods wholesaling subsector, while distributor surveys describe cross-industry use cases rather than wage-weighted tasks in 423830. I map the large sales, customer-service, office, and inventory cohorts to quote intake, order entry, product search, forecasting, pricing, invoicing, and routine communication, while assigning little substitution to mechanics, warehouse labor, drivers, and relationship-intensive technical selling. Broader-subsector data may understate technical sales and service in industrial machinery, biasing exposure upward.
caveats: The occupation mix is for a broader durable-goods wholesale grouping, not six-digit 423830.
Vendor case-study productivity figures may reflect successful deployments and not the average firm.
Exposure is to tasks and avoidable hiring, not whole jobs.
a.change_evidence: A 423830-specific OEWS staffing-and-wage extract combined with time-on-task diaries for inside sales, technical sales, customer service, purchasing, warehouse, and service staff, plus ERP event logs separating touchless from manually handled orders.
a.rationale: The work contains a substantial digital-information layer: sales representatives are the largest durable-goods wholesale occupation, with customer service, inventory, shipping, office, and management also prominent. AI can automate order and quote parsing, invoice reconciliation, product substitution, reordering, forecasting, and routine CRM work, but complex configuration, troubleshooting, negotiation, physical movement, and field service remain human-intensive.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real wholesale-trade output, and CBO projects real business fixed investment, neither of which measures constant-quality 423830 service-basket demand. I use their positive real-growth path but temper it for machinery-cycle volatility, tariff uncertainty, and the possibility that customers source directly. Broad wholesale output could overstate this machinery niche if digital channels disintermediate it, while investment growth could understate aftermarket parts demand.
caveats: BLS output is for all wholesale trade, not 423830.
CBO investment includes structures and intellectual property as well as equipment.
The range excludes nominal tariff-driven price changes by construction.
d5.change_evidence: A product-level bridge from Census Monthly Wholesale Trade and Annual Wholesale Trade Survey sales for 423830 to end-market industrial production, machinery orders, parts versus new-equipment mix, and customer procurement-channel migration through 2031.
d5.rationale: BLS projects wholesale-trade real output rising from $2.178 trillion in 2024 to $2.833 trillion in 2034, a 2.7% compound annual rate, and CBO projects positive real business fixed-investment growth through the horizon. A five-year base near 1.08 is deliberately below a straight wholesale-output extrapolation because industrial machinery demand is cyclical and the current distributor service basket faces channel shifts.

e.bridge: The Industrial Distribution subscriber survey measures service offerings among industrial distributors, not the share of 423830 LMM firms meeting this screen's transferability and recurring-service lens. I treat repeat MRO/parts fulfillment, vendor-managed inventory, technical support, and charged ancillary services as qualifying recurring outsourced functions, then discount firms dominated by sporadic capital-equipment transactions or owner-dependent technical selling. The subscriber base may overrepresent service-oriented firms, biasing eligibility upward.
caveats: Service revenue is not identical to recurring outsourced distribution activity.
The qualifying share can vary sharply by machinery category and aftermarket mix.
The injected firm count is an estimate and may include project-oriented dealers.
e.change_evidence: A stratified 423830 firm survey or transaction database tagging revenue by repeat parts/MRO, recurring supply agreements, VMI, field service, one-time machinery sales, and owner concentration, cross-tabbed to EBITDA band.
e.rationale: Half of surveyed industrial distributors offered vendor-managed inventory, while shipping, installation, kitting, engineering, and inventory management were monetized by subsets. Still, most respondents derived 10% or less of revenue from explicit services, so eligibility rests mainly on recurring outsourced procurement and fulfillment rather than standalone service revenue; capital-project-heavy dealers may not qualify.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 423830 ($35,023,418,914) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 423830 ($296,751,854,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $807,126 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 423830); class <5: 9,710 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,439,764 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $163,701 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,739 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,939,366 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $447,906 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,544 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,514,675 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $968,118 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,540 firms, avg revenue/firm $22,775,715 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,589,599 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 682 firms, avg revenue/firm $76,547,364 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,703,435 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 419 firms, avg revenue/firm $325,359,212 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $36,993,342 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 3,222

o.bridge: Industrial-distributor surveys measure the importance of delivery, support, inventory services, and supplier direct-channel intentions rather than the year-five operator-required quantity share for 423830. I interpret physical custody, availability, credit, local delivery, technical selection, installation, and aftermarket response as operator-required, while allocating a minority to customer self-service, supplier D2C, and software-mediated procurement. Large-company surveys may overstate near-term D2C pressure on LMM customers.
caveats: Operator-required does not mean every current manual role remains.
OEM channel conflict differs by vendor agreements and product complexity.
The D2C survey covers suppliers across distribution verticals.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement interviews and invoice-level channel data measuring the 423830 share migrating to OEM portals, marketplaces, automated replenishment, and self-service, split by standard parts, engineered equipment, VMI, installation, and emergency MRO.
o.rationale: Physical machinery and parts still require accountable sourcing, stocking, logistics, compatibility judgment, and support. Surveyed industrial distributors report strong customer emphasis on quality, on-time delivery, and service, while half offer VMI. However, 86% of surveyed suppliers expected to expand D2C investment and 72% expected D2C sales growth above 25% within two years, so some standardized purchasing can bypass an independent operator.

q.bridge: Industry articles document broad wholesale gross-margin pressure, customer buying criteria, and supplier direct-to-customer investment rather than the discounted share of implemented AI benefit retained by 423830 operators. I translate these competitive conditions into retention: initially private cost savings are partly competed away through quote pricing, renewals, and service expectations, but differentiated technical support and availability slow full pass-through. General distributor evidence may understate commoditization in standard parts and overstate it in engineered systems.
caveats: No source directly measures five-year AI-benefit retention.
Retention differs between proprietary engineered lines and readily comparable products.
Vendor rebates and inventory gains can change capture independently of customer pricing.
q.change_evidence: Matched branch-level gross-margin and price realization data before and after AI deployment, including contract type, renewal cadence, competitor density, customer churn, rebates, and service-level changes for 423830 firms.
q.rationale: A distributor can initially retain back-office and working-capital gains, yet buyers compare quotes and suppliers are expanding direct channels. Industrial customers rank quality, on-time delivery, and support ahead of price, which provides room to retain value when automation improves service, but long-run competition and renewal repricing should share a material portion with customers.

rho.bridge: Industrial-distribution surveys report current AI use, adoption intentions, and deployment outcomes across mixed distributor populations. I translate these into the five-year implemented share of the exposed 423830 opportunity, discounting for fragmented data, ERP integration, employee resistance, product complexity, and the gap between pilots and scaled savings. Survey respondents likely skew toward digitally engaged firms, so the bridge may overstate implementation among smaller independents.
caveats: Published surveys mix distributor verticals and company sizes.
Intent to deploy is not completed implementation.
Successful examples may suffer selection and sponsor bias.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal panel of 423830 firms measuring deployed use cases, share of orders touched by AI, labor hours removed or hiring avoided, exception rates, and realized savings by ERP maturity over 2026-2031.
rho.rationale: Adoption momentum is real but operational impact lags: one 2025 survey reported 29% AI use, only about 10% reporting positive impact, and more than 43% expecting implementation within two years; another found 90% of distributors had initiatives but only 11% had fully adopted AI. Five years allows wider rollout of embedded ERP tools, yet small-firm data quality and change management prevent most exposed work from being fully implemented.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among all U.S. employer-business owners, not completed qualifying control transfers among eligible 423830 firms. I anchor near the reported 22%, allow an upward industrial-distribution consolidation effect, and discount intentions that become closures, partial non-control transactions, family gifts without operational transfer, or failed sales. Because LMM distributors are larger and more transferable than the typical employer firm, the net direction of error is uncertain.
caveats: Owner intentions do not equal closed transfers.
Gallup is cross-industry and includes much smaller employer firms.
BizBuySell overrepresents brokered small deals and does not report a six-digit denominator.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed 423830 transactions from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, broker CRM systems, and SBA change-of-ownership loans, with denominator estimates for eligible LMM firms and flags for control, closure, and internal reorganization.
s5.rationale: Gallup found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer within five years, while 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses were owned by people age 55 or older. Wholesale transactions are demonstrably occurring, and durable-goods distributors sold through BizBuySell had a $1.8 million median revenue over 2021-2025, but stated plans and small-business-marketplace sales do not establish a 423830 completion rate.

principal_driver: Repeat quote-to-cash and inventory workflows create a sizable AI productivity surface without eliminating the physical and technical distribution role.
principal_weakness: Six-digit evidence is sparse, and OEM direct channels plus owner-dependent technical selling can sharply reduce eligibility and retained benefit.
